title: Heatstroke and dehydration in summer
excerpt: Every summer, thousands of people are caught off-guard by loo, India's scorching hot wind, and the dehydration it brings. Ayurveda understood heat illness long before modern medicine named it, and offers time-tested guidance on cooling the body and replacing lost fluids.
at_a_glance: Sip cool fluids throughout the day, before intense thirst arrives, not after.
Sattu, coconut water, and aam panna are classical Ayurvedic heat-fighters backed by the granthas.

If someone shows confusion, stops sweating despite the heat, or loses consciousness, that is a medical emergency: get to a doctor immediately. No home remedy replaces prompt medical care in a serious case.

body.heading: what summer does to the body
paragraphs: You know the feeling: the afternoon sun turns everything white, a hot wind blows dust into your face, and within minutes your mouth is dry, your head pounds, and your legs feel heavy. This is not just discomfort. In Ayurvedic terms, you are experiencing Atapa Seva, prolonged exposure to harsh solar heat, which dramatically aggravates Pitta dosha and depletes Ojas, the body's vital reserve of energy and fluid.

Ayurveda classifies summer, Grishma Ritu, as the season of peak Pitta. The sun draws moisture upward out of the earth and out of the body. The classical texts describe how Adana Kala, the half-year when the sun moves northward, progressively saps strength and depletes bodily fluids (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). The loo, known in Sanskrit texts through descriptions of Tikshna Vata, a sharp, hot wind combined with fierce solar radiation, accelerates this depletion sharply.

body.heading: signs Ayurveda recognized
paragraphs: Classical Ayurveda described a condition called Trushna, intense thirst and fluid deficit, alongside Daha, a burning sensation in the body, Murcha, fainting or loss of consciousness, and Bhrama, dizziness. The Ashtanga Hridaya describes Grishma Ritu Charya, guidelines for summer conduct, precisely because the sages understood that summer demanded active protective measures, not passive endurance (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana).

Heatstroke in modern terms corresponds to a state where the body loses its ability to regulate temperature. Ayurveda would read this as extreme Pitta aggravation combined with Vata disturbance affecting the brain and nervous system. Early signs include burning in the palms and soles, excessive thirst, a red face, scanty dark urine, irritability, and headache. If someone stops sweating despite extreme heat, feels confused, or loses consciousness, the condition has become a medical emergency.

body.heading: everyday cooling habits from the granthas
paragraphs: The Ashtanga Hridaya recommends that in summer one should drink cool, sweet, light liquids frequently rather than waiting for intense thirst to arrive (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). This is sound advice: by the time you feel very thirsty, you are already moderately dehydrated. Sip water, coconut water, or diluted buttermilk throughout the day.

Sattu, roasted chickpea flour stirred into cool water with a pinch of black salt and cumin, has been used across North India for generations as a heat-fighting drink. The Bhavaprakasha, which extensively documents grains and their properties, praises roasted gram as cooling and strength-giving (Bhavaprakasha, Dhanyavarga). Coconut water is light, sweet, and naturally contains electrolytes that mirror what sweat removes from the body. Aam panna, raw mango drink, is another traditional preparation that helps the body hold on to sodium and water.

Caraka Samhita advises avoiding direct midday sun, resting in cool and shaded spaces during peak hours, and wearing light, loose, white or light-coloured clothing (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). Wetting the head and feet with cool water, applying sandalwood paste to the forehead, and sleeping in well-ventilated rooms are all described as summer essentials. The texts also warn against vigorous exercise, heavy food, and alcohol in summer, all of which generate internal heat and worsen fluid loss.

Food in summer should be light, slightly sweet, and liquid-rich. Think curd rice, khichdi with plenty of water, fresh fruits like watermelon and cucumber, and simple dals. Avoid very spicy, salty, or oily foods that stoke Pitta. The Bhavaprakasha describes certain herbs, including dried amla and nagarmotha, as cooling and Pitta-pacifying, and these have long been added to summer drinks and preparations across Ayurvedic practice.

body.heading: when to see a doctor
paragraphs: The guidance above is general information for everyday summer wellness. It is not a diagnosis and is not a substitute for medical care. If you or someone with you shows any of the following signs, treat it as a medical emergency and seek help immediately: no sweating despite extreme heat, body temperature that feels very high to the touch, confusion or slurred speech, loss of consciousness, seizures, rapid or weak pulse, or very dark urine with no output for several hours.

Children, the elderly, people with diabetes or heart disease, and those who work outdoors are at much higher risk. A qualified doctor or vaidya should be consulted for any heat illness that does not resolve quickly with rest, shade, and fluids. Early heatstroke can become life-threatening within hours, and there is no home remedy that replaces prompt medical attention in a serious case.

try_this_today.title: Sattu cooling drink
try_this_today.description: Stir 2 tablespoons of roasted chickpea flour (sattu) into a glass of cool water. Add a pinch of black salt, a squeeze of lemon, and half a teaspoon of roasted cumin powder. Drink once or twice during the hottest part of the day.

try_this_today.title: Sandalwood forehead paste
try_this_today.description: Mix a small amount of sandalwood powder with cool rose water to form a thin paste. Apply to the forehead and temples. It cools on contact and helps ease headaches from heat exposure.

try_this_today.title: Wet cloth cooling for mild overheating
try_this_today.description: If someone feels moderately overheated but is alert and responding normally, place a damp cool cloth on the forehead, back of the neck, and wrists. Move them to shade or a cool room and offer small sips of cool water or coconut water every few minutes. Note: this is for mild heat discomfort only. If the person is confused, has stopped sweating, or loses consciousness, seek emergency medical help immediately rather than attempting home measures.

try_this_today.title: Avoid peak hours outdoors
try_this_today.description: Plan outdoor activity before 10 am or after 5 pm. If you must be outside between noon and 4 pm, wear a cotton dupatta or cap over your head, carry a water bottle, and rest in shade regularly.

try_this_today.title: Light summer meals
try_this_today.description: Eat curd rice, moong dal khichdi, or simple fruit meals during very hot days. Avoid fried snacks, very spicy curries, and alcohol, which all increase internal heat and worsen fluid loss.

ingredients.name: Cumin
ingredients.description: Roasted and added to sattu drinks; considered cooling and digestive in summer preparations.

words_to_know.definition: The summer season in the Ayurvedic calendar, characterized by intense heat and peak Pitta aggravation.
One of the three doshas, governing heat, transformation, and metabolism; naturally high in summer.
The body's vital essence or reserve of immunity and strength, depleted by heat, stress, and poor diet.
Intense, pathological thirst described in Ayurvedic texts as a key sign of fluid deficit and heat illness.
A burning sensation in the body, particularly in the palms, soles, or chest, associated with Pitta excess.
Fainting or loss of consciousness, described in Ayurvedic texts as a consequence of extreme heat and depletion.

sources: Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, seasonal regimen (Ritucharya) · Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Grishma Ritu Charya (summer conduct) · Bhavaprakasha, Dhanyavarga (grains section), properties of roasted gram
disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
